moduleTitle: Service Procedure — Routine & Periodic Hard-Floor Cleaning
summary: A repeatable service procedure for routine and periodic cleaning of the common school and childcare hard floors — vinyl/PVC, tile and grout, and sealed concrete — from dry soil removal through correct dilution, mopping or auto-scrubbing, spot and grout detail, to rinse, recovery and drying. Every product choice is child-safe and SDS-governed, dilution and any timings come from the product label/SDS/TDS rather than memory, and the floor is isolated and signed while wet.

steps.title: Plan the job and schedule around the children
steps.detail: Confirm the scope from the current contract specification and separate routine work (light, frequent cleaning to keep floors presentable and safe) from periodic work (deeper, intermittent cleaning). Schedule cleaning around occupancy — before children arrive, after they leave, or into term breaks and deep-clean windows for heavier periodic work. Prepare a SWMS/JSEA for the task, confirm each worker holds the correct state or territory Working With Children check plus any site child-safe induction, and confirm access and timing with the site contact rather than assuming a window is clear.

steps.title: Set up SDS, chemicals register and PPE
steps.detail: Obtain and read the current Safety Data Sheet (SDS) for every product before it comes on site — 'current' means reviewed within the last 5 years, and each SDS states its last-reviewed date. Enter each hazardous chemical in a maintained hazardous chemicals register (model WHS Regulation 346) kept accessible at the workplace, and correctly label every container including any bottle or bucket you decant into. Fit the PPE the SDS specifies (gloves, eye protection, appropriate footwear). Any mains-powered equipment must carry a current AS/NZS 3760 test-and-tag and be run on a 230 V / 50 Hz, 10 A GPO through an RCD/safety switch.

steps.title: Remove dry soil first (sweep / dust-mop / vacuum)
steps.detail: Dry particulate soil — grit and dust tracked in on shoes — is the bulk of what lands on a school or childcare hard floor, and a large share of it lifts off before any water touches the surface. Sweep, dust-mop or vacuum the whole area thoroughly, working edges, corners and under-desk zones. Removing grit up front stops it being ground into a vinyl finish or smeared through your cleaning solution, and it makes the wet steps faster and more effective.

steps.title: Identify the floor and select a child-safe product
steps.detail: Match the product to the floor and the soil, and choose the lower-tox option suited to an occupied, child-occupied space rather than the harshest chemistry available. For vinyl/PVC and sealed concrete carrying a finish, a neutral-pH cleaner protects the seal for routine work. For greasy or heavier periodic soils on tile and sealed concrete, alkaline hard-floor chemistry such as Actichem Defender SV (SDS AP672) helps lift oily soil so it can be rinsed away. Reserve stronger chemistry for periodic deep cleans scheduled outside child contact. Read each SDS for hazards, PPE and handling before use; if an intended product has no current SDS, obtain it from the supplier first.

steps.title: Dilute correctly and prepare the solution
steps.detail: Dilute strictly to the ratio on the product label/SDS/TDS — never stronger 'to be sure', because over-concentration raises both the hazard and the residue left behind. Use dilution control or accurate metering rather than a guess, and note that the dilution yield and coverage rate (m²/L) are product-specific figures found on the label/TDS, not something to quote from memory. Decant into a correctly labelled container, and start ventilating the space (open windows, position RCD-protected air movers) before you begin.

steps.title: Isolate and sign the wet work zone
steps.detail: A wet floor is both a slip hazard and, until rinsed, a chemical-contact hazard. Before any solution goes down, physically separate the work zone with barriers, sign it clearly, and keep children and pets out. In a childcare setting treat physical isolation as the control — children may not read or heed a sign — and route cords and hoses so they are never a trip hazard or within a child's reach.

steps.title: Apply, dwell and agitate
steps.detail: Apply the solution evenly across an area you can realistically work before it dries, and let it dwell for the time the label/SDS/TDS specifies so the chemistry loosens the soil before agitation. Agitate with the right tool for the area and floor: a flat mop or a mains/battery auto-scrubber (scrubber-dryer) for open vinyl, sealed concrete and tiled runs; for grout, pre-wet the porous lines, then use Dirty Grout Demon brushes for detail and edges and the Hydroforce SX-7 spinner head to clean and recover across open tiled floor in one contained pass. Confirm the tool suits the floor type and condition before you start.

steps.title: Treat spots and grout detail
steps.detail: Work localised spots and stained grout before or alongside the general clean, testing any spotter in an inconspicuous area first and following its own SDS, since spotters often have different handling requirements from the main cleaner. Grout lines are porous and hold greasy and particulate soil below the surface — this detail work is what actually lifts that trapped soil out, rather than sliding a flat mop over the top of it.

steps.title: Rinse thoroughly
steps.detail: Cleaning is only half done until the suspended soil is flushed out. Rinse the floor — especially grout lines — with clean water to lift the loosened soil and any chemical residue clear of the surface. If in doubt, an extra rinse pass is cheaper than a callback, because leftover residue dries as a film that dulls the floor, holds new soil and can change how the surface feels underfoot.

steps.title: Recover the dirty solution and manage waste water
steps.detail: Recover the rinse and dirty solution rather than letting it dry back onto the floor — use the auto-scrubber's recovery, a wet vacuum or careful pick-up. Good recovery is what leaves grout genuinely cleaner and tile residue-free. Dispose of waste water responsibly per the SDS disposal section and the site's rules: interior cleaning waste goes to sewer, never to a stormwater drain or garden bed. For larger volumes or any trade-waste discharge, confirm requirements with the water authority.

steps.title: Dry the floor and manage airflow
steps.detail: Move air actively with ventilation and RCD-protected air movers (230 V / 50 Hz, 10 A GPO) to bring the floor back to a safe, dry state. Do not promise a fixed dry time — it depends on the floor, the volume of water applied, airflow, temperature and humidity on the day, so manage it by conditions rather than committing to a number. Keep the area isolated while it is damp, because a wet floor stays a slip and access risk until it is genuinely dry.

steps.title: Confirm slip resistance and reinstate the area
steps.detail: Cleaning and any change to a floor's surface condition can affect slip resistance. Where a contract or site sets a target slip class, verify the cleaned in-service floor against it under AS 4663 (which measures existing, in-service surfaces) — not AS 4586, which classifies new surfaces before installation. Neither standard sets a dry or re-entry time, and neither tells you when it is safe to walk on the floor. Only remove signage and hand the area back once the floor is dry, clear and safe. Consult SA HB 198 and the NCC for recommended minimum slip classifications rather than quoting a rating from memory.

steps.title: Document the clean
steps.detail: Record what you did: the method used per surface, the products used with their current SDS, the dilution followed, the equipment (with test-and-tag status), any spot or grout treatment, and any slip-resistance verification carried out. A school or childcare contract values that evidence as much as the outcome — it is what demonstrates you cleaned the floor safely and to the standard the client expects, and it answers an audit or a parent question before it is asked.

safety: As a PCBU handling hazardous chemicals, obtain and keep a current SDS (reviewed within 5 years) for every product, maintain a hazardous chemicals register (model WHS Regulation 346) accessible at the workplace, and correctly label every container including decanted bottles and buckets.

Victoria operates under its own OHS Act and Regulations rather than the model WHS framework — check WorkSafe Victoria for VIC contracts; elsewhere direct yourself to your state regulator (e.g. SafeWork NSW, Workplace Health and Safety Queensland).
Wear the gloves, eye protection and clothing the product SDS specifies; alkaline hard-floor cleaners such as Defender SV (AP672) warrant particular care with skin and eye contact.
Ventilate while cleaning and drying; keep the Poisons Information Centre number 13 11 26 to hand and know the SDS first-aid response before you start.
A wet floor is a slip and access hazard — isolate and sign the work zone and keep it off-limits until dry; route cords and hoses clear of walkways.
Run only current AS/NZS 3760 test-and-tagged mains equipment (auto-scrubbers, air movers, chargers) on a 230 V / 50 Hz, 10 A GPO protected by an RCD/safety switch.
Prepare a SWMS/JSEA for the task and hold a White Card where site access requires it.
childSafe: Select lower-tox products suited to occupied, child-occupied rooms rather than the harshest option; reserve stronger chemistry for periodic deep cleans scheduled outside child contact.
Dilute only to the label ratio — no stronger — to limit both hazard and chemical residue left where children sit and play.
Physically isolate and sign wet or freshly cleaned floors and keep children and pets out; in childcare, a barrier beats a sign because children may not read or heed it.
Schedule cleaning around occupancy — before or after hours, or into term breaks — and coordinate re-entry with the centre; ventilate throughout.
Keep equipment, cords, hoses and chemicals secured and out of reach of children at all times.
Supporting the centre to control infection risk and avoid unnecessary chemical exposure helps it meet NQS Quality Area 2 (children's health and safety).
Workers doing child-related work generally require the correct state/territory Working With Children check, plus any site-specific child-safe induction.
standards: AS 4663 — slip resistance of existing (in-service) pedestrian surfaces; the correct basis to verify a cleaned floor against a target slip class.
AS 4586 — slip resistance classification of new pedestrian surfaces before installation (distinct from AS 4663; not used to re-verify a cleaned in-service floor).
SA HB 198 and the National Construction Code (NCC) — recommended minimum slip classifications by location type; consult directly rather than quoting a rating from memory.
AS/NZS 3760 — in-service safety inspection and testing of electrical equipment (test-and-tag) for mains auto-scrubbers, air movers and chargers.
Model WHS Act and Regulations (Safe Work Australia), incl. Reg 346 hazardous chemicals register — noting Victoria's separate OHS Act and Regulations; GHS-classified SDS.

Education and Care Services National Law and National Regulations / National Quality Standard Quality Area 2 (children's health and safety), overseen by ACECQA — the centre's obligation, which your methods should support.

ANSI/IICRC S100 is the recognised standard of care for textile (carpet) cleaning, not hard floors; it is guidance that supports judgement, not law. This course is not IICRC-accredited and awards no IICRC CECs.
documentation: Current SDS for every product used (each within 5 years), held in an accessible hazardous chemicals register (Reg 346).
Method statement per surface (vinyl/PVC, tile & grout, sealed concrete), including dilution followed and dwell per label.
Correct state/territory Working With Children checks for each worker, plus any site child-safe induction record.
Equipment AS/NZS 3760 test-and-tag records for mains machines and chargers.
Any AS 4663 slip-resistance verification records where a target slip class applies.
Cleaning schedule mapped to routine vs periodic scope and confirmed against the current contract specification.
productAvailability: Actichem Defender SV (SDS AP672), alkaline hard-floor cleaner — Australian-made (Actichem, QLD); available through the CCW catalogue in Australia. Availability checked 8 July 2026.
Dirty Grout Demon brushes and Hydroforce SX-7 spinner head — available through the CCW catalogue in Australia. Availability checked 8 July 2026.

Auto-scrubbers, air movers and chargers — specify Australian 230 V / 50 Hz, 10 A GPO models (or battery machines with 230 V chargers), current AS/NZS 3760 test-and-tag. Availability checked 8 July 2026.
Neutral-pH maintenance cleaner for finished vinyl and sealed concrete — obtain the current SDS from the Australian supplier before adding it to your register.
contentBodyHtml:
Routine & periodic hard-floor cleaning

This procedure covers the common school and childcare hard floors — vinyl/PVC, tile and grout, and sealed concrete. It runs the same repeatable sequence every time, because a consistent method gives a consistent finish and makes any problem easy to diagnose. Throughout, dilution and any dwell or drying timings come from the product label, Safety Data Sheet (SDS) and Technical Data Sheet (TDS) — never from memory.

  1. Plan and schedule around the children. Separate routine work from heavier periodic work, schedule around occupancy or into term breaks, prepare a SWMS/JSEA, and confirm Working With Children checks, site induction and access before you start.
  2. Set up SDS, register and PPE. Read a current SDS (within 5 years) for each product, enter it in an accessible hazardous chemicals register (model WHS Reg 346), label every container including decanted ones, fit the PPE the SDS specifies, and confirm mains equipment is AS/NZS 3760 test-and-tagged on a 230 V / 50 Hz, 10 A GPO through an RCD.
  3. Remove dry soil first. Sweep, dust-mop or vacuum thoroughly — most soil on a school floor is dry grit that lifts before any water touches the surface.
  4. Identify the floor and choose a child-safe product. Neutral-pH cleaner protects the finish on vinyl/PVC and sealed concrete for routine work; alkaline chemistry such as Actichem Defender SV (SDS AP672) lifts greasy periodic soil on tile and sealed concrete. Choose the lower-tox option for occupied rooms.
  5. Dilute correctly. Dilute strictly to the label/SDS/TDS ratio — never stronger — using dilution control or metering; dilution yield and coverage (m²/L) are product-specific figures on the TDS. Decant into a labelled container and start ventilating.
  6. Isolate and sign the wet zone. Barrier off and sign the work zone, keep children and pets out, and route cords and hoses clear of walkways.
  7. Apply, dwell and agitate. Apply evenly, dwell per the label, then agitate: flat mop or auto-scrubber for open floor; pre-wet grout and use Dirty Grout Demon brushes for detail and the Hydroforce SX-7 spinner head to clean and recover across open tiled floor.
  8. Treat spots and grout detail. Work spots and stained grout with the right chemistry, testing in an inconspicuous area first and following each spotter's own SDS.
  9. Rinse thoroughly. Flush loosened soil and residue clear of the surface, especially from porous grout lines; an extra rinse pass beats a callback.
  10. Recover and dispose of waste water. Recover the dirty solution by auto-scrubber, wet vacuum or careful pick-up; dispose to sewer per the SDS — never to stormwater or a garden bed.
  11. Dry the floor. Move air with ventilation and RCD-protected air movers, and manage drying by conditions. Do not promise a fixed dry time, and keep the area isolated while damp.
  12. Confirm slip resistance and reinstate. Where a target slip class applies, verify the in-service floor under AS 4663 (not AS 4586, which classifies new surfaces). Remove signage only when the floor is dry, clear and safe.
  13. Document the clean. Record the method per surface, products with current SDS, dilution, equipment test-and-tag status and any slip verification.

Safety

  • As a PCBU, keep a current SDS (within 5 years) for every product, maintain a hazardous chemicals register (Reg 346), and label all containers including decanted ones.
  • Victoria uses its own OHS Act and Regulations — check WorkSafe Victoria; elsewhere use your state regulator (SafeWork NSW, Workplace Health and Safety Queensland).
  • Wear the SDS-specified gloves, eye protection and clothing; take particular care with alkaline cleaners such as Defender SV (AP672).
  • Ventilate throughout; know the SDS first aid and keep the Poisons Information Centre number 13 11 26 to hand.
  • A wet floor is a slip and access hazard — isolate and sign it until dry.
  • Run only current AS/NZS 3760 test-and-tagged mains equipment on a 230 V / 50 Hz, 10 A GPO protected by an RCD/safety switch.

Child-safe practice

  • Select lower-tox products for occupied rooms; reserve stronger chemistry for periodic deep cleans outside child contact.
  • Dilute only to the label ratio to limit hazard and residue where children sit and play.
  • Physically isolate and sign wet floors and keep children and pets out — a barrier beats a sign in childcare.
  • Schedule around occupancy and coordinate re-entry with the centre; ventilate throughout.
  • Keep equipment, cords and chemicals secured and out of reach.
  • This work supports the centre's NQS Quality Area 2 obligations for children's health and safety.

Standards

  • AS 4663 — slip resistance of existing, in-service surfaces (used to verify a cleaned floor against a target class).
  • AS 4586 — slip resistance of new surfaces before installation (distinct from AS 4663).
  • SA HB 198 and the NCC — recommended minimum slip classifications by location; consult directly rather than quoting a rating.
  • AS/NZS 3760 — test-and-tag for mains auto-scrubbers, air movers and chargers.
  • Model WHS Act and Regulations incl. Reg 346 (Safe Work Australia); Victoria's separate OHS framework; GHS-classified SDS.
  • Education and Care Services National Law / NQS Quality Area 2 (ACECQA). ANSI/IICRC S100 is the recognised carpet standard of care only; this course is not IICRC-accredited and awards no IICRC CECs.

Document this

  • SWMS/JSEA for the task.
  • Current SDS for every product in an accessible register (Reg 346).
  • Method statement per surface, with dilution and dwell followed.
  • Working With Children checks and site induction records.
  • AS/NZS 3760 test-and-tag records for mains equipment.
  • Any AS 4663 slip-resistance verification records.
  • Cleaning schedule confirmed against the current contract specification.
